title: Guide for considering influenza testing when influenza viruses are circulating in the community | Influenza (Flu) | CDC
text:

Antiviral treatment is clinically most beneficial when started as close to illness onset as possible. Influenza vaccine effectiveness is moderate and so a history of current season influenza vaccination does not exclude a diagnosis of influenza. 
Signs and symptoms of uncomplicated influenza vary by age, underlying health conditions, and immune function. Common signs and symptoms include fever with nonproductive cough or other respiratory symptoms, often with myalgias or headache. Fever is not always present, especially in premature and young infants, immunocompromised and immunosuppressed persons, and especially in older adults. Note that some persons may have atypical presentations – especially infants (e.g., sepsis-like syndrome) and older adults (e.g., confusion). 
Complications associated with influenza can vary by age, immune status, and underlying medical conditions. Some examples include worsening of underlying chronic medical conditions (e.g., congestive cardiac failure, asthma, or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease exacerbation); lower respiratory tract disease (pneumonia, bronchiolitis, croup, respiratory failure); invasive bacterial co-infection; cardiac (e.g., myocarditis); musculoskele
